abstract:
This study examines Operation Streamline, a zero-tolerance immigration enforcement program initiated in 2005 that mandates the federal criminal prosecution of all unauthorized border crossers. Drawing on court observations and interviews with judicial and law enforcement stakeholders across four border jurisdictions, the paper evaluates the program’s efficacy, legal standing, and systemic impact. The findings suggest that Operation Streamline has fundamentally overwhelmed the federal judiciary along the U.S.-Mexico border, shifting limited resources away from the investigation of violent crimes, drug smuggling, and human trafficking toward the processing of low-level migrant workers. 

The analysis further demonstrates that the program likely fails as a deterrent; recent declines in border apprehensions correlate more strongly with U.S. economic shifts than with criminal "zero-tolerance" policies. Furthermore, the study highlights critical legal concerns, noting that the "assembly-line justice" necessitated by massive caseloads—characterized by en masse plea hearings and restricted access to counsel—violates the U.S. Constitution and Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure. The paper concludes that the administration should terminate Operation Streamline, restore prosecutorial discretion to U.S. Attorneys, and return the management of first-time border crossings to the civil immigration system to preserve the rule of law and prioritize national security.
